author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
title: The First Breviate of Cœlestius.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: “First of all,” says he, “he must be asked who denies man’s ability to live without sin, what every sort of sin is,—is it such as can be avoided? or is it unavoidable?

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: If it is unavoidable, then it is not sin; if it can be avoided, then a man can live without the sin which can be avoided.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: No reason or justice permits us to designate as sin what cannot in any way be avoided.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Our answer to this is, that sin can be avoided, if our corrupted nature be healed by God’s grace, through our Lord Jesus Christ.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: For, in so far as it is not sound, in so far does it either through blindness fail to see, or through weakness fail to accomplish, that which it ought to do; “for the flesh lusteth against the spirit, and the spirit against the flesh,” so that a man does not do the things which he would.
